financial.entity: BNP Paribas Securities (Asia) Ltd - For Colas
financial.note: Colas S.A. (France) is a global leader in road construction and holds 31.09% of TASCO as of 2026; provides technical expertise and access to international tenders.
Holds 23.07% equity; the two companies share historical roots and administrative synergies despite operating in entirely different sectors.

supply_chain.entity: Global Heavy Crude Suppliers (e.g., Middle East/South America)
supply_chain.note: Asphalt is a byproduct of heavy crude refining; supply disruptions in April 2026 (Strait of Hormuz) have pushed Brent premiums to $15/bbl over WTI, squeezing TASCO's input costs.
Domestic revenue is tied to the Thai government's infrastructure budget for road maintenance and new highway construction projects.
TASCO exports high-grade asphalt to regional markets; performance depends on the 2026 infrastructure spend in Vietnam, Indonesia, and Australia.

equity_cross_holdings.entity: South East Asia UK (Type C) Nominees

competitors.note: Competition in the international bitumen trade, particularly in the Asia-Pacific and African export corridors.
Fragmented competition for local Thai provincial road maintenance contracts.

macro_commodity_factors.logic: April 2026 forecasts peak at $96/bbl; rising oil prices increase raw material procurement costs and shipping freight rates, compressing margins.
The 2026 AGM (held April 9) confirmed a shift toward supporting domestic demand to offset weaker 1H26 export volumes.
TASCO is a net exporter; a weak Baht (trading ~33.80 in April 2026) boosts export revenue but inflates the cost of imported heavy crude.
Supply chain chokepoints in April 2026 have increased 'Cost of Goods Sold' for its international distribution fleet.
